critic_raw:
This CNN vs. traditional features experiment for cancer cell detection in microscopy has a clean overall design but contains two significant methodological issues. First, the preprocessing specifies that pixel normalization statistics (mean, std) are 'computed on the entire dataset before splitting.' This is a data leakage error: normalization using validation and test set pixel statistics contaminates the training preprocessing pipeline. While the practical impact on image normalization may be modest compared to leaking label-correlated statistics, it is still a methodological violation that should be avoided by computing statistics from the training split only.

Second, the model selection and retraining strategy has a well-known problem. After selecting hyperparameters via the validation set, both models are retrained on the combined training + validation set (9,500 images) before final test evaluation. This is a legitimate practice but requires careful implementation: retraining on a larger dataset with the same hyperparameters may produce a different model than the one selected (since optimal regularization strength, learning rate, and early stopping epoch often change with dataset size). The design does not address how hyperparameters are adjusted, if at all, when training on the combined set.

Third, while the stratified split by hospital site and class label is appropriate, the design does not address patient-level stratification. Multiple images from the same patient (common in biopsy datasets) appearing in both training and test sets inflates apparent generalization performance significantly. This is a well-documented issue in medical imaging benchmarks and must be explicitly prevented.

Finally, the sensitivity as primary metric creates an asymmetry: model selection uses 5-fold cross-validation on the training set with sensitivity as the criterion for logistic regression, but CNN uses validation sensitivity (no cross-validation). This inconsistency in model selection procedure may favor the CNN.
all_issues_raised: Data leakage: normalization statistics (mean, std) computed on the entire dataset before splitting, exposing preprocessing to validation and test pixel distributions
Patient-level leakage: stratification by hospital site and class does not prevent multiple images from the same patient appearing in both training and test sets
Retraining after model selection uses unchanged hyperparameters on a larger combined dataset — optimal hyperparameters shift with dataset size, but no adjustment protocol is specified
Asymmetric model selection: logistic regression uses 5-fold cross-validation on training set while CNN uses held-out validation set, creating an inconsistent comparison of selection procedures
Sensitivity-only primary metric without pre-specifying the operating threshold: threshold is implicitly determined by model output range rather than a fixed clinical specificity target

Preprocessing data leakage: normalization statistics for both CNN and baseline features are computed from the full dataset before splitting, incorporating validation and test distribution information into training preprocessing

Hyperparameter tuning asymmetry after retraining: the CNN uses validation-set early stopping while logistic regression uses 5-fold CV within the training set — different effective procedures despite the claim of equivalent tuning

Potential label leakage via segmentation masks: if expert-annotated masks implicitly encode cancer status (through annotation quality or completeness), shape features derived from masks may carry label information
Consensus re-labeling of 500 randomly selected images is biased toward easy cases; a random sample misses the ambiguous borderline cases where labeling noise most impacts model comparison
No test for site-level confounding in the primary analysis: per-hospital accuracy is reported as a diagnostic but not used to adjust or bound the primary sensitivity estimate
Data leakage: pixel normalization and handcrafted feature standardization statistics computed on entire dataset before splitting, contaminating training-phase normalization with test-set information

Patient-level leakage risk: if multiple images per patient exist (multiple slides or crops), random image-level splitting allows the same patient to appear in both training and test sets, inflating test performance

Retraining on combined train+validation set after hyperparameter selection may produce a model whose behavior diverges from the validated configuration, undermining the validity of the validation-based selection

Sensitivity as primary metric without a pre-specified acceptable specificity threshold allows models to achieve high sensitivity by over-predicting positives — the trade-off is not operationally constrained

The 500-image consensus re-labeling subset is randomly selected, not risk-stratified — if label disagreements are more common for challenging cases, random selection may miss the most confounded subpopulation
